target: WIN-028 detail_evidence kernel paragraph — Devil's Sea latitude anchoring (carry-over H5 from c6/c7 reviews, ISS-1938/1941/1954)
holes_addressed: WIN-028.c7-H5 (carry-over from c6 H5): Devil's Sea latitude stated as ~25°N without anchoring to source; folklore spans 25–35°N per Berlitz 1974 (centroid ~30°N).
verification_summary: The H5 hole was closed before this analyst pickup. Commit 7b6eef39 (workspace-sync 2026-05-12T06:05:41Z) had already operator-edited data/wins.json:WIN-028.detail_evidence to anchor the Devil's Sea coordinates with Berlitz 1974 attribution and to make explicit that the geometric argument is latitude-independent. The c8 curmudgeon review (WIN-028.c8.json, reviewed_at 2026-05-12T06:43:14.636Z, lines change_summary + holes_found) explicitly confirms this: "Devil's Sea anchored to Berlitz 1974, vague-reading paragraph replaced 50° heuristic with pure-unfalsifiability framing, WMM 2025 URL anchored" — i.e. H5/H6/H7 all landed in the same commit cluster. The c8 review surfaces three new minor holes but none reopen H5. Current text in data/wins.json reads: "the Devil's Sea (folklore-described as spanning ~25–35°N — Berlitz 1974 places it south of Honshu toward Iwo Jima and Guam with the typical centroid near 30°N; we use 25°N here as the southern edge to give the dome's same-latitude framing every benefit of the doubt; ~137°E) really are near-opposite in longitude. The geometrically meaningful quantity here is the longitude separation, which is invariant across the full 25–35°N folklore band; nothing in the argument below depends on the latitude choice." This is stronger than the c7-H5 recommendation, which offered either anchoring OR a longitude-only pivot; the current text does both.
current_text_excerpt: the Devil's Sea (folklore-described as spanning ~25–35°N — Berlitz 1974 places it south of Honshu toward Iwo Jima and Guam with the typical centroid near 30°N; we use 25°N here as the southern edge to give the dome's same-latitude framing every benefit of the doubt; ~137°E) really are near-opposite in longitude. The geometrically meaningful quantity here is the longitude separation, which is invariant across the full 25–35°N folklore band; nothing in the argument below depends on the latitude choice.
kernel_of_truth: The dome model identified a real geographic coincidence: Bermuda Triangle (~25°N, 71°W) and the Devil's Sea (folklore-described ~25–35°N, ~137°E) really do share matched mid-northern latitudes and near-opposite longitudes. The 25°N latitude pick is defensible (southern edge of the folklore band) and the longitude separation argument is invariant across the band — the analyst review steelmans rather than dismisses this geographic observation.
why_kernel_doesnt_save_claim: Anchoring the latitude does not change the verdict; the 28° gap (15.6% deviation) remains ~3× the dome's own WIN-029 5.3% confirmation threshold, no mechanism is published (the agonic-line steelman fails per IGRF-13/WMM 2025), and the disappearance-rate signal is absent (NOAA/Lloyd's/USCG). The H5 fix is a citation-anchoring improvement, not a verdict-changing argument.

anticipated_objections.objection: Why is the analyst writing a verification EXP for a hole already closed? Isn't that wasted work?
anticipated_objections.response: The decider routed EXP-308/309/310 (three duplicate issue-IDs for the same hole — ISS-1938, ISS-1941, ISS-1954) to the analyst after the patch had already landed on the FUSE side via operator edit; the curmudgeon c8 review then confirmed closure. The tracker entries themselves are the canonical signal that an analyst response is needed (per Mode 1 procedure). The lightweight verification-only EXP records the closure for the decider to mark all three tracker items complete in one M1 sweep, and references the c8 review so the audit trail is intact. The alternative — letting three pending tracker items rot until the next curmudgeon cycle re-flags them — is the worse outcome.

anticipated_objections.objection: Doesn't this duplicate the c8 review's patches_landed evidence?
anticipated_objections.response: The c8 review documents the closure as observed by curmudgeon at 2026-05-12T06:43Z. This EXP closes the loop on the analyst-side tracker entries created by the decider's M3 carry-over from c7 (before c8 ran). The two records serve different surfaces: c8 is curmudgeon's adversarial verification of the text, this EXP is the analyst's tracker-clearing record that decider's M1 picks up. No content duplication — only cross-reference.

cross_refs.duplicate_resolution_note: EXP-309 and EXP-310 target the identical hole (Devil's Sea latitude) for the same WIN. Recommend decider M1 sweep close all three in one transaction, citing this EXP-308 verification record and the c8 curmudgeon review.

summary_for_decider: EXP-308 is a verification-only artifact. The c7-H5 hole (Devil's Sea latitude unanchored) was already closed by commit 7b6eef39 prior to this analyst pickup; c8 curmudgeon review (2026-05-12T06:43Z) confirmed closure. Current data/wins.json:WIN-028.detail_evidence kernel paragraph anchors Berlitz 1974 AND establishes latitude-independence — stronger than the c7-H5 recommendation required. EXP-309 (ISS-1941) and EXP-310 (ISS-1954) are duplicate issue-IDs targeting the identical hole; recommend M1 sweep close all three with reference to this EXP and c8 review. No data/wins.json edit required from decider — text already correct.
